sentences.text: My name is Sol Einhorn, S-O-L, E-I-N-H-O-R-N.
My Jewish name is Zelig Einhorn.
I was born in a village named Moisei.
Moisei is located in Transylvania, in Romania.
Moisei is a-- an area where the capital for Moisei is Sighet.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Sighet is more like Albany.
And around Sighet, we had, I would say, an awfully-- about 100, 100, 120 villages and towns.
And one of this villages, where I was born in Moisei.
I have grown up there.
I was born there.
Well, as I remember, we had-- in the Moisei, we had three shuls.
We had-- up, we called it upstairs shul, middle shul, and downstairs shul.
That's three synagogues.
And we had 300 Jewish families [NON-ENGLISH], we called.
And they split up between the three shuls.
We had-- the town had a-- a mikveh.
We had, ah, nine Jewish butchers, butcher shops.
We had a [NON-ENGLISH].
And the life was around it, were around the shul and the mikveh.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Moisei was unique.
And most of the towns were more like-- the villages and towns were on the same basis.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- and for what I remember in Moisei is, the rabbi, [NON-ENGLISH], the intellectuals would-- well, one thing, everybody had in common.
[NON-ENGLISH].
From the intellectual, from the rabbi, from the intellectual, to the [NON-ENGLISH], to the shoemaker, everybody had a [NON-ENGLISH].
And they came by Friday.
They was washed up, went to mikveh, washed up and went to shul.
I was born in 1928, October.
No.
He was in business.
He was a cattle dealer, lumber and cattle.
We speak, in the house Yiddish.
We spoke Yiddish and Romanian.
Oh, well, I-- well, most of the people in my town belonged to, one way or the other, to Hasidic or to some kind of rabbi-- a rabbi, not some kind.
I would say a rabbi.
If it was a [NON-ENGLISH] or the-- or [NON-ENGLISH], or [NON-ENGLISH], every-- somehow, the majority belonged to, one way or another, to the-- to a rabbi, to a [NON-ENGLISH] rabbi.
No, no.
No, no, but I have my grand-grandfather was a [NON-ENGLISH] in-- in the area.
And he lived in Moisei.
He didn't practice over here, but he was a [NON-ENGLISH], was a Talmud-- a rabbi, and he wrote [NON-ENGLISH].
Matter of fact, I own-- he wrote five ciphers.
And I own some of them, but [INAUDIBLE].
And lately, we found an-- he-- he went away to-- to Palestine, in that time, in the late 1800s, to die in-- in Israel.
And he died in Tsefat.
And they found some of his writings in Yiddish.
And we went ahead, and we translated it in-- in Hebrew.
And we had a lot of-- quite a few [NON-ENGLISH].
And the cipher that's called [NON-ENGLISH].
This we pre-printed it, printed 1,000 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] books, [NON-ENGLISH], and-- [NON-ENGLISH].
Not for profit-making, but we printed them for-- well, I-- we consider, my cousins in Israel consider, it's more like a-- [NON-ENGLISH], all about being.
And everything what you put them out or make a [NON-ENGLISH].
And it's very well expressed and written.
His comments are terrific.
We printed it, not for profit.
It was a feeling that we had money, a tzedakah fund that we established through the family in Tel Aviv.
And that money is-- we lend it to people in need, but interest.
And that money is put back.
We borrowed money from that, from the family fund, from an aunt that left money.
And that money, we-- we-- we printed and created this-- the cipher.
The money the-- so the cipher will be sold.
It's sold, already.
It's in the United States.
Some of the seforim stores have it, where they bought it for-- and they selling it.
We are not making a profit, only on cost, like a loan.
And the money goes back to that fund.
About my father?
Yes.
He would travel out of town, once in a while, to a rabbi.
Not if I remember.
Well, an-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you're asking when I was a-- a child?
My daily life when I-- I remember, if I go back, when I was six, seven years of age, when I started cheder.
And on the same time, cheder started before me, before-- and then, when I got to be six or seven, I enlisted to a-- a Romanian school.
So I went a half a day in a Romanian school and a half a day in cheder.
Well, the gentile ran the school, many times we had-- we sat in the school, separate.
There were four-- four lines.
We was-- and I remember the first grade.
We were about 70, 60, 70 kids, boys and girls.
And we sat separate.
The Jewish boys had one line four in a seat, a big line.
And-- and the gentile had separate line together.
And the girls had a separate line together, in the same class, first class.
We had one teacher.
I even remember his name.
Was the first grade.
And he had-- well, the reason I remember his name, he had all type of sticks at his desk-- a thin stick, a heavy stick, maybe a half a dozen sticks.
And anybody didn't behave, we got the hand with the stick.
Or even, he just hit on the head too, if he got mad.
To keep discipline in the school.
And learning-- very interesting.
I mean, just imagine 60, 70 kids to learn arithmetic.
So remember arithmetic, the way we learned is he started, and the whole class sang it along.
[SINGING] One and one is two.
Two and two is four.
Four and four is eight.
And that, we continue until 10.
And then we had a half an hour, after learning that, of-- words, we were writing.
I talk of some kids to write the numbers, the names, and so on.
Outside [NON-ENGLISH], we got along with the gentile kids, not bad.
But maybe got into a fight, like kids do.
I remember there was a kid.
I got him in a fight, and-- who called me [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH], that's Jew.
Go to Palestine.
Didn't say Israel or the [INAUDIBLE].
And at that time, I didn't know much, what-- what he meant, go to Palestine.
And that was an insult to me, and I-- if he'd only told me that, I hit him.
Then he hit me back, and that's the way it continued.
And what I remember is, another-- another thing that me and my father used to deal with, until he had friends, gentile.
Got along-- well, my grandfather was born in the area and there, and grand-grandfather.
And they've grew up together.
They got along, and they dealt in business together.
On-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I remember the Christmas.
It came Christmas Eve.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They had them-- they had a church.
We had the three shuls, and the gentiles had a big-- a big church.
And somehow, there was a certain respect for the-- each other, remain, until it came, Christmas Evening.
Christmas night, I was told-- remember, I was a kid-- that they went into church.
And the-- and the Pope-- not the Pope, the priest told them that a Jew told them the story about Christmas, and that the Jews killed Jesus.

And by going out, about 12:00 at night, the youngsters-- not the older, my father's age and so on-- the young, 18 and 20, when they walked out from the church, 12:00 at night, and-- they threw stones in Jewish houses, threw stones in the synagogue.
And that happened that night, that usually at Christmas night.
And then it was quiet and continued-- life was continued.
I would say every year, yes, yes.
And the gentiles didn't do much about it, didn't make a big fuss.
It was a common.
And the story was going, when the-- when the priest told them that the Jews had killed Jesus, then they walked to the door, like make-believe.
We'll go out and kill the Jews.
Well, they killed Jesus.
And then he yelled out, children, children, hold it.
Hold it, hold it!
Come back.
Now mind, we were told.
My father, the friends told them of what's going on.
And they-- hold back, come back.
This is not the Jews, what killed Jesus.
That's many, many hundreds years ago, thousands of years ago.
And this is-- this is good Jews, and they didn't kill Jesus.
And they called them back.
The injunction was, already, given to the young people when they threw the stones.
Yeah.
Yeah, most of the-- most of the fights between gentile and-- and the Jewish kids were mostly the differential.
We were Jewish, and they were gentile-- the kids in school.
No.
Well, I didn't have my own business.
My father, he should rest in peace, was in business.
And my older brothers, two older brothers and me, we worked along with my father.
No, nothing in particular.
Well, as far as-- as far as newspapers in-- in Moisei, in the town, most of the villages or towns in that area, we had one-- one or twice, a newspaper a week, only.
We had no radio.
There were no-- no radios, no electricity-- a very primitive and sweet life, good life, I would consider, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] whatever it was.
Well, in 1933 I don't remember much.
Two years later, I remember, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1936, '35, already had started the pogroms in Romania.
And it came-- the government changed in a short period of time.
It was called the Iron Guard, [NON-ENGLISH], if I remember.
And they came in power for a short time.
And the king, Michael-- Gustav was still king.
And-- and at the time, at that time, they threw Jews out of the trains.
And it happened that from my town, Moisei, was a young man, coming from-- from Sighet or someplace, on the train.
We had a train come into town once a day.
So-- once a day or every two days.
I don't remember, exactly.
And he was thrown out of the train, and a hand-- he broke a-- a hand.
And they had to amputate, take off his hand.
And at the same time, I remember they had a program, a-- an anti-Semitic, when the Iron Guard came in power.
Must have heard, the papers, even the United States, at times, if you look.
I was told that the gentiles in next town, where my grandfather lived, it's only 10 kilometers away, Bosha.
And from Bosha, you-- there were the big mountains.
You just went over.
You had to go over the hills to go to Bukovina, it was called.
Bosha, they had a-- the gentiles, in the evening, somehow they poured-- poured gasoline on every house-- Jewish home.
And-- in that town-- it wasn't a village.
It was a town, was bigger than a village-- more-- more Jewish people, and more gentile live there.
And they put it on fire, and it burned down completely.
Every house, they burned.
And I had my uncle lived in the Bosha, and my grandfather, and an aunt.
And they were in underwater for-- they had a running water, and this is where they were living, near the water, near the river.
All the Jewish people went on the river there, and they built whatever before Shabbat.
And was a-- big Thursday and Friday.
The Shabbos was a very, very important way of life to us.
My father, my-- my mother, my brothers, my relatives-- in the whole, Moisei, we didn't have nothing.
And we put together food, and challahs, and bread.
And we put on a wagon, and I and my brother-- and I was a kid.
I was only seven, eight years.
It sticked in my mind.
I'll never forget.
Then we went to Bosha.
We brought them the challahs and bread for Shabbos.
And then I had an uncle in Bukovina.
He was a very wealthy man.
And he came back-- came down after that, and he helped.
And with my father's help, and-- to create, build back a house for my grandfather and uncle in Bosha.
Yes, yes.

Well, well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was, number one-- I mean, as far as the religion in-- in our life, my father, my grandfather, my mother, my-- my father-- number one, my father took me to mikveh every Monday and Thursday, when I was a child.
Friday, that was the big day.
It was mikveh.
We had a steam-- a big steam room in the town.
And especially, as we didn't have showers, and we didn't have water in the-- it felt pretty good, as a child, if I look back, to go into a hot mikveh in the wintertime.
And I was going, not only in the wintertime, even the summertime, in the steam rooms-- one big steam room.
And then-- then they had Fridays.
They could have brought a leu or two leu.
They brought, with a barrel, water, into the top.

And they poured the hot water and they-- and if-- if some people took a second to use the same water, what somebody else used, it was good enough for them, paid only half a leu and use the same water to bathe, to wash up before Shabbos.
And that-- the mik, no question about it.
On [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Shabbos was a very important event in the town.
Number one, we made-- we had a bakery.
And we had-- we made cholent.
That's a famous dish, by-- the Jewish orthodox, or non-orthodox, or orthodox people.
And the cholent was taken into the bakery, to the baker, a top.
And that way would be some meat, and so on.
Somehow, I always wondered how come they didn't mix up.
Everybody recognized their own top from the-- from the bakery.
And for Saturday-- we had a [INAUDIBLE] in town.
And Saturday, they went-- the kids went, and brought home, and they knew which top to bring on the cholent.
On-- and like I said, my father had a [NON-ENGLISH].
So did the [NON-ENGLISH], and so did the rabbi.
Everybody had that uniform, [NON-ENGLISH].
We were-- in the family, we were nine children, and Father, and Mother.
We had-- we were six brothers and three sisters.
Oh, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was no way, where to go, what-- for where to leave.
I mean, no, no.
Yeah.
Well, the trouble started in-- I would say 1940 or '41.
The trouble started in '36, when they, for a short period, when they were in the towns and throw Jews out of the trains in Romania, and anti-Semitism.
And they were writing, in-- in different butcher's stores, [NON-ENGLISH], Jewish meat is cheap, or something translation in Romanian.
I felt, as a child at that time-- that time we had in school, and we had fights with kids, and anti-Semitism started very, very strong, really.
In 1940 or '41, we were under the Romania-- Romanian government.
And Hitler, somehow, now what I understand, gave back the piece of land to the Transylvania, to the Hungarian.
Well, the Hungarians always blamed that that belonged to them, that somehow.
One day, I remember the Hungarian soldiers, gendarmes, marched into town and took over this whole area, and became Hungary.
And the Romanian gendarmes moved out from the town and became Hungary.
And that-- that year, or a year or two years, was very, very rough, as far as food, clothing and food, food.
We suffered a lot about hunger.
It started a hunger, till when-- took a little while, and then we got back to ourselves, the family.
The family, we got back with food.
We start dealing more, opened up a little more to-- a-- do business in the-- by the Hungarians, under the Hungarian regime.
And it started getting better.
My father was dealing, back with the cattles, and with the lumber.
And then that was in the wintertime.
In the summertime, he used to lease out from the government.
He did it in-- but the Romanian he had to lease out mountains from the government, and the spring, by March.
And then big mountains, few mountains, where you-- a lot of acres, and acres, and acres, and then he made a deal with the gentiles in every town.
Well, they wanted to work their land, and for the-- for the animals to have the hay, and have the potatoes, and have other things.

And they-- the cattle, [INAUDIBLE], or the horses, or the cows, what gave milk, or the sheep, what they were milking, he couldn't keep them in the summertime, and do the work, and keep them, and feed them.
And he didn't have the hay, neither.
Well, he had to grow it in the summertime and get it ready for the winter.
So my-- my father, shall rest in peace, made deals.
Let's say if a-- if a gentile had 100 sheep, and he had 5-- 5 cows, and they gave milk, so he made a deal for the four months-- three, four months.
He should be able to work the land.
He paid him to keep, in pasture, all this cattles and-- and so on.
And he took it up in the-- in the mountains.
And he had people, and he-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] worked with him.
And they milked the--
